articles.title: 🦀 OpenClaw: The Runaway AI Assistant
articles.description: An intense week for a new open source TypeScript project: 150k stars on GitHub, hordes buying Mac Minis to run it, two rebrands (it was originally Clawdbot), and an ecosystem of projects the agents use autonomously (e.g. an entire social network). Another example of TypeScript at the heart of the AI boom.
articles.author: Peter Steinberger

articles.title: ⁉️ How Not to Parse Numbers in JavaScript
articles.description: Why use a proper locale-aware API to parse numbers when you can hand-roll a maze of string splits, separator swaps, and implicit type coercions that silently break on edge cases?
articles.author: Remy Porter (The Daily WTF)

articles.title: 📉 Node.js 16 to 25 Benchmarked Version-by-Version
articles.description: The jumps in performance in various areas are striking (with Node 25, especially), with other areas getting more modest gains.
articles.author: RepoFlow

articles.title: Explicit Resource Management in JavaScript
articles.description: You can use using for deterministic cleanup, calling Symbol.dispose/asyncDispose at scope exit without try/finally. A small fix for leaks and forgotten teardowns in streams, observers, locks, and similar APIs.
articles.author: Matt Smith

articles.title: The History of C# and TypeScript with Anders Hejlsberg
articles.description: GitHub interviewed the creator of both C# and TypeScript about his career, why TypeScript was created in the first place, some internal Microsoft politics, as well as the ongoing Go port of the TypeScript compiler. There's a video of the full interview, as well as 'seven learnings' boiled down in written form.
articles.author: GitHub

articles.title: Heat.js 5.0: A Flexible Heat Map Rendering Solution
articles.description: Generate customized interactive heatmaps (think GitHub contributions graph), or render heatmaps as lines and bar charts. The site is packed with demos to enjoy. GitHub repo.
articles.author: William Troup

articles.title: 🕒 Croner 10.0: Cron-Style Triggers and Evaluation
articles.description: Trigger functions on any cron schedule using cron syntax. It can also evaluate cron expressions to give you a list of upcoming times. v10.0 brings full OCPS (Open Cron Pattern Specification) 1.4 compliance and even more scheduling options.
articles.author: Hexagon

articles.title: 🗓️ DayFlow: A Full Calendar Component for React
articles.description: A React-only feature-rich calendar component with drag-and-drop, multiple views, and all the usual GCal-style richness. Its infinite scrolling feature is nifty. GitHub repo.
articles.author: DayFlow Contributors

articles.title: Tsonic: A TypeScript to C# Transpiler
articles.description: The idea is for creating native executables that run on .NET. I’ve not tested it as I’m not in that ecosystem but it’s an interesting idea.
articles.author: Jeswin

articles.description: The popular JSBin collaborative JavaScript pastebin went down for a few days and Remy Sharp shares the war story of what happened.
🔒 The OpenJS Foundation has shared an annual report covering its efforts in securing the JS ecosystem.
Angular is changing components' default change detection strategy.
Both Astro and Svelte have shared monthly roundups of what's new.
Node.js v25.6.0 (Current) – async_hooks can now skip Promise hooks to reduce overhead. Plus, URL parsing now supports Unicode 17.
jQuery UI 1.14.2 – The legacy UI library gets its first update in over a year and now works with jQuery 4.0.
Bun v1.3.8 – Say hello to native Markdown parsing.
Astro 5.17, ESLint 10.0.0-rc2, tRPC v11.9.0, Lexical 0.40.0, Reka UI 2.8 (formerly Radix Vue)

articles.title: The Too Early Breakpoint
articles.description: Ahmad thinks we're collapsing to single-column layouts way too soon. With CSS Grid and Container Queries, we can create smarter breakpoints, fluid transitions, and intermediate views that actually use the space we've got.
articles.author: Ahmad Shadeed

articles.title: Solving 'Shrinkwrap': A New Experimental Technique
articles.description: Roman tackles a CSS problem "deemed impossible". That is, shrinkwrapping an element with auto-wrapped content. His solution combines anchor positioning and scroll-driven animations, and he posits it could work as a future native feature. A superb explainer with plenty of interactive examples.
articles.author: Roman Komarov

articles.title: No-Hassle Visual Studio Code Theming: Building an Extension
articles.description: One of our most linked frontend authors over the years tackled the task of making his own VS Code theme using modern tooling and shares his insights. He prototyped the palette with Shiki, then wrapped the theme in a minimal extension to preview changes live while tuning scopes.
articles.author: Zell Liew

articles.title: Drawing Connections with CSS Anchor Positioning
articles.description: A good tutorial that explains the power that anchor positioning unlocks. Roland notes it “feels like layout-aware CSS” and is “especially powerful for UI patterns where relationships matter more than strict placement”. There’s a CodePen demo here to illustrate what’s possible.
articles.author: Roland Franke

articles.title: Performance-Optimized Video Embeds with Zero JavaScript
articles.description: “What if I told you, you could lazy-load videos on interaction, without any JavaScript, even above the fold, with only native HTML and CSS?”
articles.author: Stefan Bauer

articles.title: Throttling Individual Network Requests in Chrome DevTools
articles.description: Here’s a look at a new developer tool (in Chrome 145+) to throttle or block specific network requests. This granular control (found in the Request conditions tab) will help improve debugging/testing, particularly sluggishly loading dependencies.
articles.author: Gasperowicz & Pfaffe (Chrome Developers)

articles.title: Introduction to the New <geolocation> Element
articles.description: Support is limited to Chrome for now, but Manuel takes us on a quick tour of this promising new HTML element.
articles.author: Manuel Matuzovic

articles.title: Nice Select
articles.description: Some fun experimentation here pushing the customizable select element to its extremes. Watch the attached video to get an idea of what’s possible – support is limited right now, but it's really good food for thought!
articles.author: Adam Argyle

articles.title: How the Same Content Always Has Multiple Different Versions
articles.description: An interesting accessibility point here looking at the differences the same content can often have. The visuals here really help make the point.
articles.author: Diana Khalipina

articles.title: Combobox vs. Multiselect vs. Listbox: How To Choose
articles.description: Looks at how each is different, what purpose they serve, and choosing the right one.
articles.author: Vitaly Friedman

articles.title: Sweetfont: Find Fonts By 'Vibe'
articles.description: A fun, browser-based tool to surface options available on Google Fonts (from the over 1900 choices available nowadays) based on various characteristics, such as ‘playful’, ‘loud’, ‘ornate’, ‘heavy’, etc. Dial in what you want.
articles.author: Peter Cooper

articles.title: EaseMaster: A Tool for Visualizing and Generating Code for Easing Animations
articles.description: Includes a Cubic Bezier Editor and a Spring Physics Engine and has support for exporting CSS transitions, Tailwind, Framer Motion, and GSAP. Repo.
articles.author: Satish Kumar

articles.title: minimal-pwa: A Minimal Set of Files & Config for a Progressive Web App
articles.description: A minimal bare-bones starting point for creating a PWA, with a manifest.json and service worker code to trigger the install on supported browsers.
articles.author: Chris McCormick

articles.title: WarGames Terminal Fonts: A Font Made from the Text Terminal Displays in WarGames
articles.description: A retro typeface based on terminal sessions in the popular 1980s movie. The author created this after noticing “sufficient information present on-screen” in the movie’s text terminal displays.
articles.author: Michael Walden

articles.title: Flexboxle: A Daily Flexbox Puzzle
articles.description: Here's a fun Wordle-style puzzle where you're challenged to enter the correct CSS Flexbox property values in order to reach the goal output. A good way to practice (or teach) Flexbox concepts and better understand the mental model at play.
articles.author: Flexboxle
